guide/festivals.json.title: Festivals & events
guide/festivals.json.subtitle: 42 celebrations through the year — pick a day to see what's on.

festivals.name: New Year's Day
festivals.description: National public holiday marking the beginning of the Gregorian calendar year with celebrations across India.

festivals.name: Winter Carnival Auli
festivals.description: A vibrant winter festival in India's only ski resort celebrating snow sports, adventure activities, and mountain culture.

festivals.name: Makar Sankranti
festivals.description: A harvest festival celebrated with kite flying, bonfires, and traditional sweets across India, marking the sun's transition into Capricorn.

festivals.name: Lohri
festivals.description: A Punjabi winter festival celebrated with bonfires, traditional foods like til and gur, and community gatherings.

festivals.name: Republic Day
festivals.description: National public holiday celebrating India's constitution and democratic republic with parades and flag hoisting ceremonies.

festivals.name: Vasant Panchami
festivals.description: The spring festival celebrated with yellow flowers, kite flying, and honoring the goddess Saraswati, marking the season of harvest.

festivals.name: Hornbill Festival
festivals.description: Nagaland's premier cultural festival showcasing tribal traditions, indigenous crafts, music, dance, and local cuisine.

festivals.name: Sumo Festival (Nagaland)
festivals.description: A unique wrestling festival in Nagaland blending traditional Indian wrestling with Japanese sumo elements.

festivals.name: Maha Shivaratri
festivals.description: A major Hindu festival honoring Lord Shiva with night-long vigils, prayers, and devotional celebrations across temples.

festivals.name: Holi - Festival of Colors
festivals.description: India's most exuberant festival celebrating the victory of good over evil with colored powders, bonfires, and community feasts.

festivals.name: Camel Fair (Pushkar)
festivals.description: One of the world's largest camel fairs featuring colorful camel trading, folk performances, and spiritual ceremonies on sacred grounds.

festivals.name: Gangaur Festival
festivals.description: A spring festival celebrating womanhood and fertility with processions, folk dances, and traditional Rajasthani costumes.

festivals.name: Good Friday
festivals.description: A Christian public holiday commemorating the crucifixion of Jesus Christ, observed with church services and prayers.

festivals.name: Ambubachi Mela
festivals.description: An ancient fertility festival at Kamakhya Temple in Assam attracting pilgrims who celebrate the goddess's menstrual cycle.

festivals.name: Rath Yatra (Chariot Festival)
festivals.description: Lord Jagannath's grand chariot procession in Puri, one of Hinduism's most important pilgrimages drawing millions of devotees.

festivals.name: Buddha Purnima
festivals.description: A national holiday celebrating Buddha's birth with special prayers, meditation, and temple processions across Buddhist communities.

festivals.name: Eid ul-Fitr
festivals.description: A national Islamic holiday marking the end of Ramadan with prayers, feasts, and community celebrations.

festivals.name: Eid ul-Adha
festivals.description: A national Islamic holiday commemorating Prophet Ibrahim's sacrifice with prayers and communal feasts.

festivals.name: Hemis Festival
festivals.description: Ladakh's most important Buddhist festival celebrating Guru Padmasambhava's victory over evil with masked dances and colorful costumes.

festivals.name: Independence Day
festivals.description: National holiday celebrating India's independence from British rule with flag hoisting, patriotic events, and parades.

festivals.name: Janmashtami
festivals.description: A major Hindu festival celebrating Lord Krishna's birth with midnight prayers, fasting, and playful celebrations in homes and temples.
festivals.location: Pan-India, vibrant in Maharashtra and UP

festivals.name: Onam
festivals.description: Kerala's grandest festival celebrating the harvest and the return of a mythical king with boat races, flower carpets, and feasts.

festivals.name: Ganesh Chaturthi
festivals.description: A ten-day festival honoring Lord Ganesha with grand processions, elaborate clay idols, and communal gatherings in Maharashtra.

festivals.name: Pushkar Fair (Livestock & Cultural)
festivals.description: A colorful livestock trading fair combined with cultural performances, spiritual gatherings, and desert tourism.

festivals.name: Dussehra
festivals.description: A major Hindu festival celebrating the victory of good over evil with Ramlila performances, Durga Puja, and effigy burning.

festivals.name: Diwali (Deepavali)
festivals.description: India's Festival of Lights celebrating the victory of light over darkness with lamps, fireworks, sweets, and family gatherings.

festivals.name: Guru Nanak Jayanti
festivals.description: A national Sikh holiday celebrating the birth of Guru Nanak with prayers, processions, and communal feasts.

festivals.name: Chhath Puja
festivals.description: An ancient Hindu festival primarily in Bihar and UP where devotees fast and worship the sun god with colorful offerings.

festivals.name: Christmas
festivals.description: A national public holiday celebrating the birth of Jesus Christ with church services, family gatherings, and festive decorations.

festivals.name: Goa Carnival
festivals.description: A vibrant pre-Lenten celebration featuring parades, floats, music, and dancing reflecting Goa's Portuguese colonial heritage.

festivals.name: Vijayadashami (Bengal)
festivals.description: Durga Puja, Bengal's grandest festival celebrating goddess Durga with elaborate pandals, processions, and cultural programs.

festivals.name: Konkanastha Marathi Brahmin Shivaratri
festivals.description: A regional Hindu festival honoring Lord Shiva celebrated with fasting and temple prayers in Maharashtra communities.

festivals.name: Khimji Fair
festivals.description: A traditional cattle and camel fair in Rajasthan showcasing local culture, livestock trading, and folk performances.

festivals.name: Baisakhi
festivals.description: A harvest festival marking the Punjabi New Year with traditional music, dance, and community celebrations.

festivals.name: Mahavir Jayanti
festivals.description: A national holiday celebrating the birth of Jain prophet Mahavir with temple processions and spiritual gatherings.

festivals.name: Thrissur Pooram
festivals.description: Kerala's grandest temple festival featuring decorated elephants, music, and fireworks at Vadakkumnathan Temple.

festivals.name: Kumaon Holi
festivals.description: A unique regional Holi celebration in Uttarakhand featuring folk songs, traditional music, and regional culinary traditions.

festivals.name: Khajuraho Festival of Dances
festivals.description: A classical dance festival held at the UNESCO World Heritage temples featuring multiple Indian classical dance forms.

festivals.name: Navratri
festivals.description: A nine-day festival celebrating the divine feminine with Garba and Dandiya dances, especially vibrant in Gujarat.

festivals.name: Hampi Festival
festivals.description: A cultural festival at the ancient Vijayanagara empire ruins featuring classical music, dance, and heritage celebrations.

festivals.name: Pongal
festivals.description: A Tamil harvest festival celebrating the sun god with traditional cooking, cattle racing, and colorful decorations.

festivals.name: Rann Utsav
festivals.description: A winter festival celebrating the white Rann salt desert with cultural performances, adventure activities, and traditional crafts.

guide/history.json.title: A Short History of India
guide/history.json.subtitle: From its beginnings to today — 13 eras that shaped it.

items.label: Indus Valley Civilization
items.summary: A sophisticated urban culture flourished across the Indus River basin with planned cities and undeciphered script.
items.text: One of the world's earliest major civilizations, the Indus Valley culture built remarkable cities like Harappa and Mohenjo-daro with grid-like streets, advanced drainage systems, and standardized weights and measures. Archaeological evidence suggests a trading network stretching from Mesopotamia to the Persian Gulf, yet the civilization mysteriously declined around 1300 BC for reasons still debated by scholars. The people left behind beautiful seals, pottery, and figurines that hint at a complex society, though their written script remains undeciphered. This culture represents India's deepest historical roots and influenced later Vedic traditions.

items.label: Vedic Age
items.summary: Indo-Aryan peoples composed the Vedas, foundational Sanskrit texts that shaped Hindu philosophy and the caste system.
items.text: As Indo-Aryan groups migrated into northern India, they created the Rigveda and other sacred texts through oral tradition, which became the bedrock of Hindu thought and practice. During this period, the basic framework of Vedic society crystallized, including the division of people into varnas or castes, which would profoundly structure Indian society for millennia. The Vedic period saw the transition from nomadic pastoralism to settled agriculture, with communities along the Ganges River becoming increasingly organized. By the later Vedic age, powerful kingdoms emerged in the Gangetic plains, setting the stage for the great empires to come.

items.label: Age of Enlightenment
items.summary: Siddhartha Gautama and Mahavira founded Buddhism and Jainism, revolutionizing Indian spirituality and challenging Vedic orthodoxy.
items.text: In the 6th century BC, two spiritual reformers emerged who would reshape Indian civilization: Siddhartha Gautama, who became the Buddha, taught a path to enlightenment through ethical living and meditation, while Vardhamana Mahavira established Jainism with its extreme asceticism and doctrine of non-violence. Both religions rejected the Vedic priesthood and caste hierarchy, offering salvation to all people regardless of birth, and their rise reflected growing urbanization and merchant class influence. The Buddha's teachings spread rapidly, establishing monasteries and attracting followers across the subcontinent. This period also saw the rise of philosophical schools like the Upanishads, which explored the nature of reality and consciousness with profound intellectual depth.

items.label: Mauryan Empire
items.summary: Chandragupta Maurya built India's first great empire, with Ashoka later spreading Buddhism across Asia through moral conquest.
items.text: Chandragupta Maurya seized power after Alexander the Great's retreat from India, establishing a centralized state with sophisticated administration, standing armies, and extensive trade networks reaching Egypt and Greece. His grandson Ashoka became history's greatest Buddhist emperor, famously renouncing conquest after witnessing the horrors of war and instead spreading the dharma through edicts carved into rocks and pillars across his vast realm. Under Ashoka, Buddhism flourished as a state religion and missionary faith, with missionaries sent to Sri Lanka, Central Asia, and beyond, making it an international religion. The Mauryan court was a center of learning and art, exemplified by Ashoka's polished stone capital with its famous lion motif, which later became India's national emblem.

items.label: Gupta Empire
items.summary: India's classical golden age witnessed unparalleled achievements in mathematics, astronomy, literature, and art under the Guptas.
items.text: The Gupta dynasty, establishing power in 320 AD, presided over a renaissance of Hindu culture and learning that earned the era the title 'India's Golden Age.' This period produced mathematical genius Aryabhata, who calculated pi accurately and understood that the earth rotates; it saw the playwright Kalidasa create timeless dramas and the poet Bhartrihari compose verses of enduring wisdom. Gupta emperors were great patrons of art and architecture, funding the construction of magnificent temples and the creation of exquisite sculpture, while Hindu philosophy and mathematics advanced dramatically with the development of the decimal system and zero. The empire controlled vast territories and maintained prosperous trade routes, bringing wealth and cultural exchange across the Indian Ocean to the Middle East, Southeast Asia, and beyond.

items.label: Delhi Sultanate
items.summary: Muslim Delhi sultans ruled northern India for three centuries, blending Islamic and Hindu cultures and leaving architectural marvels.
items.text: Beginning with the Turkish general Muhammad of Ghor, a succession of Islamic sultans established kingdoms across northern India, introducing Persian governance structures and Islamic law to the subcontinent while creating a syncretic Indo-Islamic culture. These rulers built magnificent monuments including the Qutub Minar, India Gate, and early versions of Delhi itself, showcasing an architectural fusion of Islamic and Indian styles that became distinctly Indo-Islamic. The sultanate period saw intense military campaigns, the forced conversion and voluntary adoption of Islam by significant populations, and the flourishing of Urdu language and Sufi mysticism. Despite political fragmentation and periodic chaos, this era fostered cultural exchange and produced a unique composite civilization where Hindu and Muslim traditions interpenetrated in art, music, literature, and daily life.

items.label: Mughal Empire
items.summary: Mughal emperors created India's most splendid empire, uniting much of the subcontinent under Islamic rule while patronizing arts and learning.
items.text: Babur, a Central Asian warrior-king, invaded India and established the Mughal Empire, which reached its zenith under his descendant Akbar, whose enlightened policies granted religious tolerance and administrative equality to Hindus and other non-Muslims. The Mughals were supreme patrons of architecture, producing the Taj Mahal under Shah Jahan as perhaps the world's most perfect building, along with magnificent forts, mosques, and gardens that still define India's cultural landscape. Akbar's court became a center of art, literature, and learning where Persian, Arabic, Hindi, and Sanskrit traditions flourished together, while his administrative genius created a bureaucratic system that held together a diverse, vast empire. The Mughal period saw India achieve enormous wealth and population, with cities like Delhi and Agra rivaling the greatest in the world, though ultimately rigid caste systems, periodic religious conflicts, and regional rebellions weakened the empire's latter days.

items.label: British Colonial Rule
items.summary: The British East India Company conquered India piecemeal, transforming it into a colonial possession with exploitative economic policies.
items.text: After the Battle of Plassey in 1757, the British East India Company gradually extended control over India through military conquest and political manipulation, eventually subjugating the entire subcontinent under British Crown rule from 1858 onward. The British introduced railways, telegraphs, and English education, which ironically created an educated Indian elite fluent in the colonizers' language and steeped in Enlightenment ideals of liberty and self-determination. However, colonial rule systematically deindustrialized India, extracting raw materials and wealth to enrich Britain while impoverishing the Indian masses, transforming a prosperous manufacturing nation into a dependent agrarian economy. The British imposed racist hierarchies, disrupted traditional industries, caused devastating famines, and introduced administrative unity that paradoxically enabled Indians to imagine themselves as a single nation rather than separate kingdoms and communities.

items.label: Independence Movement
items.summary: Indian nationalists led by Gandhi and Nehru fought for independence through non-violence, organization, and civil disobedience.
items.text: The Indian National Congress, founded in 1885, gradually became a mass movement for independence, transforming from elite petition-writers into a revolutionary force under leaders like Bal Gangadhar Tilak and Lala Lajpat Rai who demanded 'Swaraj' or self-rule. Mohandas Gandhi emerged as the dominant figure after World War I, developing his philosophy of Satyagraha or non-violent resistance, which inspired millions to boycott British goods, defy unjust laws, and challenge the empire's moral authority without weapons. Jawaharlal Nehru represented the secular, modern, socialist vision of independent India, complementing Gandhi's spiritual nationalism with scientific rationalism and institutional dreaming. Through decades of protests, strikes, civil disobedience campaigns like the Salt March, and political negotiation, the independence movement forced Britain to withdraw, culminating in India's freedom on August 15, 1947.

items.label: Partition and New Nation
items.summary: India gained independence but was divided into Hindu-majority India and Muslim-majority Pakistan, causing massive communal violence and displacement.
items.text: As British rule ended, India was partitioned based on religious majorities, creating the Muslim-majority Pakistan while the Hindu-majority Indian Union emerged as a sovereign democratic nation. The partition triggered one of history's greatest human catastrophes, with approximately one to two million people killed in communal riots, Hindu-Muslim massacres, and violence as 15 million people crossed borders seeking safety in their newly designated homelands. Mahatma Gandhi, the architect of non-violence, was himself assassinated by a Hindu extremist in January 1948, a tragedy that shook the young nation but also galvanized commitment to secular, democratic governance. Despite trauma and chaos, India's founding leaders drafted the world's longest and most ambitious constitution, establishing a secular, democratic federal republic that granted universal adult suffrage to 350 million people of diverse religions, languages, and castes.

items.label: Republican India
items.summary: India built democratic institutions, pursued socialist-inspired development, and navigated wars and internal challenges as a sovereign republic.
items.text: The Indian Constitution, adopted on January 26, 1950, established the world's largest democratic federation, guaranteeing fundamental rights and abolishing untouchability despite India's ancient caste system remaining socially entrenched. Prime Minister Jawaharlal Nehru pursued state-led industrial development, building dams, steel plants, and universities to transform India into a modern nation while maintaining democratic pluralism and secular governance that protected minorities. India faced wars with Pakistan over Kashmir, fought China over border disputes, endured the trauma of Partition's aftermath, and struggled with linguistic reorganization of states, famines, and persistent poverty affecting hundreds of millions. The Green Revolution of the 1960s-70s increased agricultural productivity and reduced famine risk, while India became a nuclear power and sent rockets to space, demonstrating technological ambition despite remaining desperately poor by global standards.

items.label: Economic Liberalization
items.summary: India abandoned socialist policies and embraced market reforms, initiating rapid economic growth that created a tech boom and lifted millions from poverty.
items.text: Facing a severe balance-of-payments crisis in 1991, India's government under Prime Minister Narasimha Rao abandoned the socialist model and liberalized the economy, opening markets to foreign investment and competition while retaining democratic governance and welfare commitments. Information technology became India's global flagship, with Indian engineers and companies like Infosys and Tata Consultancy Services becoming world-renowned, while Bangalore and Hyderabad emerged as 'Silicon Valleys' attracting global capital and talent. Economic growth accelerated to six to eight percent annually, creating hundreds of millions of jobs, expanding the middle class to unprecedented scale, and lifting hundreds of millions from absolute poverty, though inequality widened dramatically. India became increasingly important in global affairs, a nuclear power recognized for technological prowess, a major IT exporter, and a rising economic power, though challenges of poverty, illiteracy, gender inequality, and caste discrimination persisted.

items.label: Modern India
items.summary: Under Prime Minister Narendra Modi, India pursues Hindu nationalism, rapid development, and great power ambitions while democracy faces pressures.
items.text: Narendra Modi's election in 2014 brought Hindu nationalism to the forefront, with the BJP government promoting 'Make in India' development schemes, ambitious infrastructure projects like the bullet train and smart cities, and an assertive foreign policy challenging Pakistan and China. India's economy has grown to become the world's fifth largest by nominal GDP and third by purchasing power parity, with a massive digital payment revolution and space program reaching Mars and the Moon, cementing India's technological arrival. However, this period has seen concerning trends including the erosion of minority protections, communal violence between Hindus and Muslims, restrictions on free speech and press freedom, and increasing polarization along religious lines that threaten the secular democratic consensus. Today's India remains a paradox: a nuclear-armed power with world-class tech companies, yet still home to 400 million people living in poverty; a thriving democracy with vibrant elections, yet with concerning authoritarian drift; and a nation of extraordinary diversity and dynamism navigating the contradictions of rapid development, religious nationalism, and the unfinished work of

guide/food.json.title: Food & Drink
guide/food.json.subtitle: How to eat well in India — customs, dishes and where to go.

items.label: Chai (Masala Tea)
items.text: The backbone of Indian daily life: black tea brewed with milk, sugar, and spices like cardamom, ginger, and cloves. Every street corner has a chai wallah (vendor) selling small cups for ₹10–20; this is the real deal, not the fancy café version.

items.label: Filter Coffee
items.text: South India's obsession: strong coffee made with a traditional metal filter, served in a steel tumbler and dabara (saucer) with hot milk and sweetened condensed milk already mixed in. Expect it thick, sweet, and absolutely essential if you're in Karnataka or Tamil Nadu.

items.label: Filter Coffee Shops (South India)
items.text: In Bangalore, Chennai, and Mysore, look for unmarked local spots where men sit on small stools drinking coffee at dawn. These are rarely tourist destinations but offer authentic experience and unbeatable prices (₹30–50).

items.label: Avoid Chain Café Culture Markup
items.text: Starbucks and hipster cafés charge 10x the price of street chai for inferior quality. Tourist areas often trap visitors into overpriced 'authentic chai experiences'—stick to actual street vendors or family-run dhabbas instead.

items.label: Chai Poured from Height
items.text: Watch vendors pour chai between containers held high apart—this aerates it, cools it slightly, and creates the signature frothy top. It's skill and tradition, not showing off.

items.label: Biryani
items.text: Rice cooked with meat (chicken, lamb, or goat), potatoes, and aromatic spices in a sealed pot so flavors steam together. Each region claims superiority—Hyderabadi biryani (with rawmeat) and Lucknowi biryani (with cooked meat) are the most famous. Get it from established restaurants or family biryani joints, never tourist traps.

items.label: Dosa
items.text: A South Indian crepe made from fermented rice and lentil batter, served crispy with sambar (spiced vegetable stew) and coconut chutney. Masala dosa has spiced potatoes inside; paper dosa is thin and crunchy. Breakfast food that works all day.

items.label: Butter Chicken [Murgh Makhani]
items.text: Tandoori chicken pieces in a creamy tomato sauce with butter and cream. It's richer and sweeter than regional curries, invented in Delhi in the 1950s, and now ubiquitous—good versions are genuinely delicious, but mediocre ones are forgettable.

items.label: Rogan Josh
items.text: Kashmiri meat curry (traditionally lamb) with yogurt, tomatoes, and warm spices like cardamom and bay leaves. It's aromatic rather than fiery, and pairs perfectly with naan or rice.

items.label: Samosa
items.text: Fried pastry pyramid filled with spiced potatoes, peas, and sometimes meat. Eaten as a snack or starter with tamarind chutney and green chutney. Street versions (₹5–10) are often better than restaurant ones; seek out vendors with constant turnover.

items.label: Chole Bhature
items.text: North Indian chickpea curry paired with fried, pillowy leavened bread. It's heavy, indulgent, and best eaten for lunch in Punjab or Delhi at old-school dhabbas where it's made fresh daily.

items.label: Idli & Sambar
items.text: Steamed rice and lentil cakes (idli) served with sambar (spiced vegetable stew) and chutneys. It's light, gluten-free, and a South Indian breakfast staple. Look for shops that make them fresh in the morning.

items.label: Spice Levels Are Real
items.text: Ask about spice before ordering. 'Medium' in an Indian restaurant is often genuinely medium—not the mild-for-Westerners version. State your tolerance clearly, and restaurants will respect it.

items.label: Vegetarian (शाकाहारी) vs. Non-Vegetarian
items.text: India has a massive vegetarian population; menus are often split into veg and non-veg sections. Many restaurants mark veg dishes with a green dot and non-veg with red. This is genuine, not marketing—respect it if you have dietary needs.

items.label: Ask for Recommendations
items.text: Staff will steer you to the restaurant's specialty and what's fresh that day. They're not trying to upsell—they're genuinely proud of their food. Follow their advice.

items.label: Bread is Separate
items.text: Naan, roti, or paratha come as sides and cost extra (₹30–80 each). Don't assume they're included. Order based on how many people are eating and how hungry you are—one bread per person is typical.

items.label: Curry Names Vary by Region
items.text: The same dish has different names in different places. What's called 'kadai chicken' in Delhi might be listed differently in Mumbai. Describe what you want (tomato-based, creamy, spiced) if the name isn't clear.

items.label: Small vs. Large Portions
items.text: Portion sizes are generous; start with fewer dishes than you think you need. You can always order more. Many first-time visitors order too much and waste food.

items.label: Street Food Vendors & Carts
items.text: The best and cheapest food in India. Pani puri, chaat, samosas, and dosa are ₹10–30. Eat where there's high turnover and crowds—freshness and food safety are guaranteed by business volume. Avoid eating alone at very empty stalls.

items.label: Dhabbas (ढाबा)
items.text: Highway or roadside restaurants, often family-run, serving simple North Indian food (dal, curries, bread). They're authentic, cheap, and reliable. The bathroom situation varies—cleanliness standards are improving but remain inconsistent.

items.label: Local Restaurants (Family-Run)
items.text: Unmarked or simply named spots serving regional cuisine to locals, not tourists. These have the best food and lowest prices (₹150–400 per person). Ask your hotel for recommendations rather than using Google Maps.

items.label: Hotel Restaurants
items.text: Mid-range to high-end, reliable hygiene, air-conditioned. Prices are 2–3x higher than local spots but food quality is consistent. Good for business dinners or when you want certainty.

items.label: Tourist Area Trap Warning
items.text: Restaurants within walking distance of major monuments (Taj Mahal, temples, beaches) charge 3–5x normal prices for mediocre food. Walk 5–10 minutes away from the main drag to find real restaurants.

items.label: Udipi Restaurants (South India)
items.text: Simple, vegetarian fast-food chains serving dosa, idli, and South Indian fare. They're fast, clean, and cheap (₹80–200). Found throughout South India and increasingly in North India too.

items.label: Lassi
items.text: Yogurt drink blended with water, sometimes sweetened with sugar or fruit (mango lassi is popular). Served cold, it's refreshing and aids digestion. Street versions are cheap (₹30–50) but carry slight food-poisoning risk; restaurant lassis are safer.

items.label: Fresh Juices & Sugarcane Juice
items.text: Freshly pressed sugarcane juice, orange juice, and fruit smoothies are everywhere. The juice is fresh but not pasteurized; eat where you see the juice being made from whole fruit or cane. Avoid pre-made batches.

items.label: Coconut Water
items.text: Fresh from the coconut, served with a straw and machete. It's hydrating, cheap (₹30–60), and sold everywhere in coastal and warm regions. Ask for a young (green) coconut, not mature ones.

items.label: Indian Beer
items.text: Kingfisher, Carlsberg, and local brands are popular and good. Beer is served cold and costs ₹100–300 depending on venue. It pairs well with spicy food.

items.label: Whiskey & Alcohol
items.text: India produces good whiskeys (Amrut, Paul John) and is a rum producer. Alcohol is available in bars, restaurants, and liquor shops (though some states have dry days or hours). Prices are reasonable at bars and restaurants but vary by state alcohol tax.

items.label: Alcohol Laws Vary by State
items.text: Some states (Gujarat, Bihar) are dry; others have restricted hours or dry days (usually Sundays). Check locally. Many restaurants serve alcohol discreetly even in semi-dry areas.

items.label: Avoid Cheap Liquor
items.text: Counterfeit or poorly regulated alcohol exists. Stick to branded bottles in bars and restaurants. Street-level cheap spirits are genuinely risky.

items.label: Right Hand for Eating
items.text: In traditional settings, eat with your right hand. Using your left hand is considered disrespectful (it's historically associated with bathroom hygiene). Using a fork and knife is acceptable in restaurants.

items.label: Removing Shoes Indoors
items.text: In temples, homes, and some traditional restaurants, remove your shoes before entering. Look for a shoe rack or follow what others do. This shows respect.

items.label: Communal Dining
items.text: Sharing food from one plate is normal and bonding. If offered a bite, it's polite to accept. However, don't share saliva (no licking spoons before offering).

items.label: No Beef in Some Contexts
items.text: Beef is religiously sensitive for Hindus; pork for Muslims. Many vegetarians avoid all meat. Always ask before assuming what's served or offering food. Never order beef casually in conservative areas or with religious families.

items.label: Tipping is Optional but Appreciated
items.text: Tipping (10–15% in restaurants) is not mandatory but welcomed. Street food vendors and dhabbas rarely expect tips. High-end restaurants may add a service charge; check the bill.

items.label: Don't Touch Sacred Food Offerings
items.text: In temples and homes, food offerings (prasad) are blessed and given as gifts. Accept with your right hand and eat immediately. Don't refuse or set it down disrespectfully.

items.label: Water & Hygiene
items.text: Tap water is not safe for tourists; drink bottled or filtered water (₹20–40 per liter). Many restaurants provide boiled water with meals. Ice in drinks can be risky—avoid in street food unless you trust the vendor's water source.

items.label: Tap Water & Raw Salads
items.text: Your stomach needs time to adjust. Avoid raw vegetables and tap water for the first week. Cooked food and bottled water reduce risk significantly.

items.label: Street Meat Dishes
items.text: Kebabs, tandoori chicken, and meat curries from street vendors are usually safe (high heat kills bacteria). The issue is spoilage if meat sits—eat where there's high turnover. Avoid meat that's been sitting in the sun for hours.

items.label: Dairy & Cream-Based Dishes
items.text: Milk in India is often not pasteurized. Paneer (cheese) and yogurt are usually safe because of acid/salt. Avoid unboiled milk and cream-heavy dishes at places without reliable refrigeration.

items.label: Street Juices & Smoothies
items.text: Fresh is great, but non-pasteurized juice can harbor bacteria. Eat where you see the juice being made fresh from whole fruit. Avoid pre-made batches sitting in containers.

guide/transport.json.title: Getting Around
guide/transport.json.subtitle: Trains, roads, taxis and how to move around India.

items.label: Book early on IRCTC
items.text: Use the Indian Railways official website (irctc.co.in) to book tickets 120 days in advance. Sleeper and AC classes fill quickly on popular routes like Delhi-Mumbai and Chennai-Bangalore.

items.label: Download RailYatri app
items.text: This app shows live train status, platform numbers, seat layouts, and even food ordering. It's more reliable than station announcements for real-time updates.
Tickets are non-refundable within 4 hours of departure, and cancellation charges increase as travel date approaches. Refunds take 5-7 business days.

items.label: Tatkal booking for last-minute travel
items.text: If regular tickets are full, Tatkal opens 24 hours before departure with a premium (10-30% surcharge). This is legitimate but expensive.
Occasional strikes by railway unions can cancel services without notice. Check news sites and railway announcements before booking critical journeys.

items.label: Metro systems in major cities
items.text: Delhi, Mumbai, Bangalore, Hyderabad, and Chennai have modern metro networks. Download city-specific apps (Delhi Metro Rail Corporation app, Mumbai Metro One) for maps and real-time delays.

items.label: Rechargeable metro cards
items.text: Buy a contactless smart card at any metro station and load it with ₹100-500. This is faster than queuing for tokens and accepted on buses too.
Women have reserved cars (first and last) on metros during peak hours; men risk ₹500+ fines for entering these. Harassment incidents do occur, so travel in groups when possible.

items.label: State and private buses
items.text: State transport corporations (APSRTC, MSRTC, BMTC) run cheap intercity buses. Book via redBus or ixigo apps to compare prices and reserve seats.
Private bus operators sometimes drive recklessly at night. Daytime journeys are safer; avoid buses without seatbelts or operated by unknown companies.

items.label: Uber and Ola dominate rideshare
items.text: Both apps operate in major cities and most towns. Ola is slightly cheaper in smaller cities; Uber is more reliable in metros. Always request via app—don't flag taxis on street in unfamiliar areas.

items.label: Use UberGo or Ola Lite for budget rides
items.text: These economy options are 30-40% cheaper than regular sedans. Drivers are rated and have police background checks.
Share rides (UberPool, Ola Share) with strangers carry higher risk. Avoid late-night shared rides, especially alone; solo UberX or Ola Select are safer.

items.label: Prepaid taxi booths at airports
items.text: At Delhi, Mumbai, and Bangalore airports, use official prepaid taxi counters rather than unmarked taxis. Fixed fares prevent overcharging.
Some drivers may claim meters are 'broken' or demand inflated rates. Refuse and use ride-sharing apps instead, which charge by algorithm and are non-negotiable.
Indians drive chaotically—no lane discipline, high speeds, and poor traffic rules enforcement. Unless you're experienced with aggressive traffic, avoid self-driving.

items.label: Hire a driver through agencies
items.text: Zoomcar, Avis, and Hertz offer self-drive rentals, but hiring a local driver (₹1,500-2,500/day via OLX or travel agents) is safer and more practical.

items.label: International Driving Permit required
items.text: Foreigners need an International Driving Permit (IDP) plus passport and visa. Local licenses are not valid.
Fines for traffic violations (speeding, no seatbelt, wrong lane) are strict—₹500-5,000. Police may demand on-the-spot payment; always ask for a receipt.
Night driving on highways is dangerous due to speeding trucks, poor lighting, and stray animals. Avoid traveling after 8 PM.

items.label: Book domestic flights early
items.text: IndiGo, SpiceJet, Air India, and GoAir compete heavily. Prices drop 4-6 weeks before departure; booking 2 months ahead often saves 30-50%.

items.label: Use Skyscanner and Google Flights
items.text: These aggregators compare all airlines and show price trends. Direct airline websites sometimes have flash sales but lack price comparison.
Budget airlines charge heavily for baggage, seat selection, and changes. Read fine print; ₹1,000-2,500 fees for 1 kg excess luggage are common.

items.label: Arrive 2 hours early for domestic, 3 for international
items.text: Security queues can be long, especially during peak hours (7-10 AM). Delayed arrival risks missing flights.

items.label: Baggage limits and restrictions
items.text: Domestic flights allow 20-23 kg checked + 7 kg carry-on. Liquids over 100 ml, sharp objects, and lighters are prohibited; violations result in confiscation.

items.label: Mumbai to Goa ferry service
items.text: Cordelia Cruises and similar operators run scenic overnight ferries (₹3,000-8,000). Book via their websites; this beats driving 12 hours and includes meals.

items.label: Backwater tours in Kerala
items.text: Houseboats and public ferries navigate Kochi and Alleppey backwaters. Public ferries are cheap (₹50-300) but slow; private tours cost ₹5,000-15,000 and are more comfortable.
Ferries operate on weather-dependent schedules. Monsoon (June-September) often causes cancellations; confirm sailings 24 hours before departure.

items.label: Island ferries in Andaman
items.text: Inter-island ferries connect Port Blair to Havelock and Neil Island (₹300-800). Book through shipping operators or travel agents; seats fill quickly in peak season.

items.label: Multi-day metro passes
items.text: Most metros offer 1-day, 3-day, or weekly passes (₹100-500) that save money vs. single journeys. Calculate your trip count before buying.

items.label: IRCTC tourist passes for train travel
items.text: Non-Indian residents can buy Indian Rail Passes (7-90 days, ₹7,000-25,000) for unlimited train travel. Available only at international airports or before arrival.
Tourist passes exclude premium trains (Rajdhani, Shatabdi); book those separately. Coverage varies by class and duration.

items.label: Cashless payments for tickets
items.text: Most ticket counters, apps, and machines accept cards and UPI (Google Pay, PhonePe, Paytm). Carrying small change is still wise for rural areas.
Refund policies vary by operator. Train refunds are slowest (5-7 days); flights and buses refund within 2-3 days if claimed immediately.

items.label: Buy prepaid SIM at airports
items.text: Jio, Airtel, Vodafone, and BSNL have counters in all major airports. Airport SIMs cost ₹500-800 but include ₹300-500 talk time and instant activation.

items.label: Identity verification required
items.text: Passport and visa are sufficient for foreigners buying SIM cards. Keep copies handy; activation takes 1-2 hours.

items.label: Jio offers best data deals
items.text: Jio's prepaid plans (₹155-699) include generous data, calls, and SMS. Validity is 7-84 days; auto-renewal is enabled by default—disable if staying short-term.
4G coverage is excellent in cities but patchy in rural areas and mountains. Download offline maps (Google Maps, OsmAnd) and cache routes before traveling.

items.label: Use eSIM if available
items.text: Some operators (Jio, Airtel) offer eSIM activation via app—faster than physical SIM cards. Requires compatible smartphone.
